    https://help.sap.com/docs/JOULE/3fdd7b321eb24d1b9d40605dce822e84/38636457acc346daa4ff7069f041a11a.html
  • *5) SAP Help Portal: Prerequisites for working with SAP products
     https://help.sap.com/doc/de3af3c0f81642dbaa4d36172ed57a72/CLOUD/en-US/79bfc83ab386450c8cd9c7937ce26a3a.pdf#:~:text=services%20tenant%20and%20within%20all,based%20application%20and%20relies%20on
  • *6) SAP Help Portal: Setup instructions for Joule Integration with SAP Cloud Identity Services
    https://help.sap.com/doc/de3af3c0f81642dbaa4d36172ed57a72/CLOUD/en-US/79bfc83ab386450c8cd9c7937ce26a3a.pdf#:~:text=groups%20Groups%20user_uuid%20Global%20User,Save%20your%20configuration

entry.title: New pilot service to help customers go live with SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition successfully
entry.content:
In the modern business world, seamless collaboration and efficient information sharing are the cornerstones of a successful enterprise. To support organizations in their digital transformation, we are introducing the SAP CQC Deployment Readiness Check service for SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition through the SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council program – designed to ensure a smooth, secure, and stable deployment experience for your digital workplace transformation.

The SAP CQC Deployment Readiness Check Service for SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition is specifically crafted to verify the key configuration aspects. By analysing configuration parameters, we help detect potential issues early, ensuring a seamless deployment of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition.

During the service, an SAP expert will log into your SAP BTP tenant and the related environment to analyze the configuration and usage. The focus will be on:

  • Verifying configuration settings to ensure correct setup and optimal functionality.
  • Validating system configurations to align with business requirements.
  • Checking configuration parameters to support seamless access to essential content and applications.

After a thorough analysis, the expert will provide detailed recommendations, including technical adjustments, best practices, and configuration optimizations, to ensure proper configuration, optimize system settings, and maintain seamless operations.

Upon completion, you will receive a remote service report outlining observations, insights on your SAP Build Work Zone configuration.

To see a short video on the ESAC program and service, please see this link.

Since this is a pilot service, availability is limited and will be provided on a first-come, first-served basis. If you are interested in availing of this service, please contact us at esac@sap.com to secure your slot.

Ensure a successful go-live and unlock the full potential of SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition with SAP’s expert guidance!

Thanks and Regards,

Robert Kelly

BTP Lead - SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: My Experience Developing Side-by-Side Applications in SAP BTP for SAP S/4HANA Cloud
entry.content:
In this post, I'd like to share my personal experience developing Side-by-Side applications using SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) in projects where I've implemented SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

The architecture I'm sharing here isn't the only way or necessarily the best approach for every scenario. Rather, it's the solution I've found effective and applied successfully across multiple projects.

Why did I choose a Side-by-Side Architecture?

I opted for this architecture mainly because it allows me to extend the standard functionalities of SAP S/4HANA Cloud without directly impacting the ERP core, thus making it easier to maintain a clean core and simplifying the management, maintenance, and evolution of developed applications.

How do I organize SAP BTP?

I usually set up separate subaccounts for each project environment:

  • Development: Where I perform initial testing and functionality development.

  • Quality (Test): Used for integrated testing before final deployment.

  • Production: A stable environment accessed by end users.

This approach has helped me keep things organized, avoid confusion, and significantly simplify the management of BTP services and instances.

Frontend using SAPUI5 and Fiori Elements

My frontend applications are typically developed using SAPUI5 or Fiori Elements, which accelerates development. I deploy these apps in the HTML5 Application Repository and publish them using SAP Build Work Zone, simplifying the management of connections to backend services.

Backend: Choosing between CAP and Java Spring Boot based on needs

For backend development, I divide responsibilities across two main application types, depending on technical requirements and project complexity:

  • CAP (Cloud Application Programming) Applications:
    I use CAP primarily for simpler OData services or functionalities with low concurrency. CAP has been practical for me due to its ease of development and seamless integration with standard SAP services.

  • Java Applications with Spring Boot:
    For scenarios that require complex processing, high concurrency, transactional logic, advanced reporting, or parallel processes, I prefer Java with Spring Boot. This stack consistently delivers excellent performance for demanding scenarios.

Combining these technologies within the same architecture allows me to select the most suitable tool for each specific need, rather than forcing one technology into unsuitable scenarios.

Shared SAP HANA Database

To simplify data management, I typically use a single SAP HANA database shared by both backend application types. A particular choice I make, perhaps less common, is not to use the default HDI infrastructure provided by CAP.

Instead, I manually create a dedicated HANA schema directly accessed by both CAP and Java applications.

This approach has advantages such as simpler database management and easier integration, but it also has some drawbacks, like losing certain automated management features provided by HDI. Still, based on my experience, the benefits have outweighed these disadvantages.

Direct Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud

Another critical aspect of my architecture is integration with S/4HANA Cloud. Here, I've applied two methods based on backend technology:

  • For Java/Spring Boot, I typically use the SAP S/4HANA Cloud SDK for Java, as it simplifies the connection and management of S/4HANA services significantly.

  • For CAP, I often import EDMX models exposed by S/4HANA Cloud directly, facilitating quick and straightforward integration with standard SAP services.

Both approaches have proven practical and effective in various contexts.

Centralized Authentication with Single Sign-On (SSO)

In most implementations, end users log in using the same authentication they use for S/4HANA Cloud. I achieve this by setting up trust relationships between SAP BTP and the Identity Provider (IDP) used by the ERP. Consequently, once logged into BTP, users can seamlessly interact with S/4HANA Cloud through transparent Single Sign-On (SSO).

This significantly improves the user experience and simplifies access and permission management.

Everything I've shared here comes directly from my practical experience implementing real projects. While this isn't the only method, it’s an architecture I've found functional, flexible, and effective. I'm always open to learning from other experiences and alternatives to continuously evolve my approach according to future needs.

I hope sharing my experience is helpful for anyone seeking a practical reference when implementing their own Side-by-Side architectures with SAP BTP and SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

Thank you for reading, and see you in my next post!

entry.title: Introducing the New Project Creation Wizard within SAP Build Lobby
entry.content:
Introduction:

We are pleased to announce the launch of an enhanced Project Creation Wizard in the SAP Build Lobby, designed to accelerate and streamline your project initiation processes. This new tool reflects our commitment to incorporating user feedback and optimizing your interaction with SAP Build. 

Background: 

To better understand user needs, we conducted extensive research involving interviews with a diverse group of users.  

A common theme in user feedback highlighted the need for a more intuitive project creation flow, starting with the user's objective before progressing to tool selection. Users expressed interest in new capabilities directly accessible from the Lobby, such as Node.js Full Stack and Java Full Stack options. 

Different Project Creation Options

Our findings guided us in redesigning the project creation process to align with user preferences and streamline the initial steps. 

What’s New? 

  • Intent-Based Navigation: The redesigned Project Creation experience prioritizes the user's goals and required tasks. Relevant project types are grouped logically to facilitate easier and faster selection, ensuring intuitive project management. 
  • Consistent Experience: In line with Fiori UX guidelines, we offer a seamless and consistent experience across the Project Creation Wizard and Extensibility Wizard. This step-by-step approach provides clarity and guidance, helping users make informed choices regarding their unique scenarios. 
  • Modern Look & Feel: Project creation options are categorized by Objectives, Categories, and Types, assisting developers in locating the right tools promptly. This organization allows for future expansion with additional capabilities. 

Next Steps: 

We value your input and invite you to share your experience with the new Project Creation Wizard. Your feedback is crucial for continued improvement. Use the "Share Product Feedback" option and click the "Give Feedback" button to let us know how this updated experience supports your professional objectives. 

Feedback

 

For more information, please visit SAP Help Documentation. 

We look forward to enhancing your project creation journey with SAP Build and ensuring our tools meet your business needs effectively. 

Already tried it? Please feel free to share your thoughts also in the comments below!

entry.title: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition
entry.content:
Were you also struggling to subscribe to the SAP Build Work Zone Standard Edition on April 26, 2025? I finally managed to add the service—here's how!

I detailed below how to make it happen.


General Process

Here are the steps we will follow:

  1. Include a service to authorize program access.
  2. Add new roles.
  3. Subscribe to our SAP Build Work Zone, Standard Edition.

Include a service to authorize program access.

  1. Let's add Cloud Identity Services

  2. Click on Go to Application



  3. Check you email
  4. Create an account for this service
  5. Navigate to Identity Providers --> Corporate Identity Providers
  6. Click on "Create," and name it (e.g., SAP Build Standard).



  7. Scroll down to the Single Sign-On section to toggle on "Forward All SSO Requests to Corporate IdP"
  8. Leave this open; we'll return shortly.

Add new roles 

This step is straightforward but can be a bit tricky:

  1. Navigate to your subaccount

  2. Go to security --> role collection

  3. Look for "Launchpad Admin", "Subaccount Service Administrator", and "Subaccount Service Viewer" 

     

  4. Assign your user to these three roles

Subscribe to SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition 

Now it's time to add the service. Please note, an additional step is required:

  1. Add the service "SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition"
  2. Return to Cloud Identity Services.
  3. Navigate to the Applications & Resources --> bundled applications.
  4. Select SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition (trial)
  5. In the trust section, toggle on "Trust All Corporate Identity."

Fingers crossed, everything should now work smoothly!

If you encounter any issues, here's an alternative approach:

  • Go to the Global Account → Booster.
  • Search for an application like "SAP Build App" and execute it.
  • If another user is created, assign the previously mentioned roles and retry.

I hope this helps you all!

Byron Carvajal

entry.title: Custom Tile Visualization & Navigation in SAP Build Workzone - Cloud foundry launchpad: Solved
entry.content:
Introduction -

SAP Build Workzone is a powerful platform for modern enterprise applications. However, during my development journey, I encountered a significant hurdle: The standard offering does not currently provide a built-in option for creating custom visualizations.  While I initially relied on official documentation and community blogs, I discovered that these resources were insufficient or focused on SAP Fiori Launchpad or SAP Cloud Portal service, etc. This blog details my approach and the workarounds I developed to bridge the gap.

The Challenge -

Overcoming a Lack of option to implement Custom visualization. It left developers like me to carve our own path in delivering the tailored user experiences our projects demanded.

Official Documentation -

This is the only official documentation available in help.sap.com which aims to explain how to implement a custom visualization. But, as it only speaks moderately about the manifests of both the file, it becomes tough to implement the solution.

A glance at the custom tile -

Both the standard tile types provided by SAP offers only basic configurable fields like title, subtitle, icon, and description. Additionally, the dynamic tile can display a single value (fetching dynamically from backend service). But both of them lacks in customizing the tile's visualization.

The 1x2 tile above is custom implemented. Its loading a custom visualization and when clicked, it navigates to the main/target application within the launchpad with personalized site settings (as expected from a launchpad tile). Below is the target app that launches on click of the custom tile. By the end of the blog, you too would be able to achieve this for your app.

Let's dive into the solution -

You need to build two apps with different IDs. One for the visualization itself and one that implements the visualization. If you already have your [target] app in your Build Workzone, you can modify its manifest and re-deploy along with its visualization app.

Custom Visualization App: 

This app will have the visual content to load on the target app's tile. The trick is in the app's controller and the manifest, where we take care of the navigation to the target app and launchpad configs.

manifest.json

The crucial section of manifest.json is sap.flp, which tells the app should be considered by the launchpad as a tile and of size 1x2.

{
    "_version": "1.65.0",
    "sap.app": {
        "id": "customtileviz",
        "type": "application",
        "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties",
        "applicationVersion": {
            "version": "0.0.1"
        },
        "title": "{{appTitle}}",
        "description": "{{appDescription}}",
        "resources": "resources.json"
    },
    "sap.ui": {
        "technology": "UI5",
        "icons": {
            "icon": "",
            "favIcon": "",
            "phone": "",
            "phone@2": "",
            "tablet": "",
            "tablet@2": ""
        },
        "deviceTypes": {
            "desktop": true,
            "tablet": true,
            "phone": true
        }
    },
    "sap.ui5": {
        "flexEnabled": false,
        "dependencies": {
            "minUI5Version": "1.132.1",
            "libs": {
                "sap.m": {},
                "sap.ui.core": {}
            }
        },
        "contentDensities": {
            "compact": true,
            "cozy": true
        },
        "models": {
            "i18n": {
                "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel",
                "settings": {
                    "bundleName": "customtileviz.i18n.i18n"
                }
            }
        },
        "resources": {
            "css": [
                {
                    "uri": "css/style.css"
                }
            ]
        },
        "rootView": {
            "viewName": "customtileviz.view.MainView",
            "type": "XML",
            "id": "customtileviz"
        }
    },
    "sap.cloud": {
        "public": true,
        "service": "customtileviz"
    },
    "sap.flp": {
        "type": "tile",
        "tileSize": "1x2"
    }
}

 

MainView.controller.js

In the visualization app's controller (shown below), I'm using the sap.ushell.Container.getServiceAsyn to use its CrossApplicationNavigation service for navigating to the target app. The shell hash takes the values of semantic object and action that is defined within the target app's manifest file.

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], (Controller) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("customtileviz.controller.MainView", {
        onInit: function () {
            const oView = this.getView();
      
            oView.addEventDelegate({
              onAfterRendering: () => {
                const mainDom = oView.getDomRef();
                if (mainDom) {
                  mainDom.addEventListener("click", () => {
                    this.navigateToAnotherApp();
                  });
                }
              }
            });
          },
      
          navigateToAnotherApp: async function () {
            try {
              const oCrossAppNav = await sap.ushell.Container.getServiceAsync("CrossApplicationNavigation");
              const sHash = "targetapp-display"; // Replace with you target app's semantic object and action
          
              oCrossAppNav.toExternal({
                target: {
                  shellHash: sHash
                }
              });
            } catch (err) {
              console.error("Navigation service unavailable:", err);
            }
          },
    });
});

 

Target App: App that you want to launch from the tile

Manifest.json of the target app is crucial where you need to set the app's intent for cross-navigation and set the visualization properties of the app. Apart from the manifest we do not need to take care of any other files in the target app.

manifest.json

{
  "_version": "1.65.0",
  "sap.app": {
    "id": "targetapp",
    "type": "application",
    "i18n": "i18n/i18n.properties",
    "applicationVersion": {
      "version": "0.0.1"
    },
    "title": "{{appTitle}}",
    "description": "{{appDescription}}",
    "crossNavigation": {
      "inbounds": {
        "intent1": {
          "semanticObject": "targetapp",
          "action": "display",
          "title": "Target App",
          "subTitle": "{{flpSubtitle}}",
          "signature": {
            "parameters": {},
            "additionalParameters": "allowed"
          }
        }
      }
    },
    "resources": "resources.json",
    "sourceTemplate": {
      "id": "@sap/generator-fiori:basic",
      "version": "1.17.3",
      "toolsId": "69ae75e3-85b4-4ffb-bfd8-b73b13f30c65"
    }
  },
  "sap.ui": {
    "technology": "UI5",
    "icons": {
      "icon": "",
      "favIcon": "",
      "phone": "",
      "phone@2": "",
      "tablet": "",
      "tablet@2": ""
    },
    "deviceTypes": {
      "desktop": true,
      "tablet": true,
      "phone": true
    }
  },
  "sap.ui5": {
    "flexEnabled": false,
    "dependencies": {
      "minUI5Version": "1.135.0",
      "libs": {
        "sap.m": {},
        "sap.ui.core": {}
      }
    },
    "contentDensities": {
      "compact": true,
      "cozy": true
    },
    "models": {
      "i18n": {
        "type": "sap.ui.model.resource.ResourceModel",
        "settings": {
          "bundleName": "targetapp.i18n.i18n"
        }
      }
    },
    "resources": {
      "css": [
        {
          "uri": "css/style.css"
        }
      ]
    },
    "routing": {
      "config": {
        "routerClass": "sap.m.routing.Router",
        "controlAggregation": "pages",
        "controlId": "app",
        "transition": "slide",
        "type": "View",
        "viewType": "XML",
        "path": "targetapp.view",
        "async": true,
        "viewPath": "targetapp.view"
      },
      "routes": [
        {
          "name": "RouteDynamicPageAnalyticalTable",
          "pattern": ":?query:",
          "target": [
            "TargetDynamicPageAnalyticalTable"
          ]
        }
      ],
      "targets": {
        "TargetDynamicPageAnalyticalTable": {
          "id": "DynamicPageAnalyticalTable",
          "name": "DynamicPageAnalyticalTable"
        }
      }
    },
    "rootView": {
      "viewName": "targetapp.view.App",
      "type": "XML",
      "id": "App",
      "async": true
    }
  },
  "sap.platform.hcp": {
    "uri": "",
    "appName": "targetapp",
    "_version": "1.1.0"
  },
  "sap.cloud": {
    "public": true,
    "service": "targetapp"
  },
  "sap.cloud.portal": {
    "targetapp-display": {
      "vizType": "customtileviz"
    }
  }
}

Changes you need to make in the target app's manifest -

1. In "crossNavigation" section I've set the app's intent i.e., semantic object and action. You have to use this same combination in the controller of the visualization app to enable the launchpad navigation to the target app. You cannot test this in preview as this service would be available only when the app loads in the launchpad.

2. In "sap.cloud.portal" I've used the same combination of "semantic object-action" to define this app's visualization to be loaded from the custom tile app. In the vizType property I have mentioned the app id of the visualization app.

Final steps:

Once you've maintained these configs in respective apps, you have to deploy them in your Cloud foundry environment/space. On successful deployment, refresh the contents in Build Workzone's Channel manager. You will only see the Target app in the content manager. The visualization app wouldn't appear in the launchpad's content manager. Do not worry! you just need to add the target app to the required role and group. And voila!! it's that simple to implement a custom visualization.

Exception and fix -

If you're using a backend service to load data in your visualization, you might get to see the tile loading with an error saying, "cannot load tile".

In that case, go to your site settings from the site directory in Build Workzone.

Under Browser settings, set the Asynchronous Module Loading as No (shown above). This should fix the tile loading issue. The same error could appear if your app has some bugs. Advising you to run the app locally to preview content before deploying.

Conclusion -

While the standard option to create custom visualizations remains unavailable, End users who are transitioning to the Cloud foundry environment are missing out on the custom tile feature they had in Neo environment. I hope this implementation helps in resolving the visualization challenges faced while migrating from Neo to Cloud foundry environment and also helps them who are just exploring the launchpad capabilities.

By tweaking this implementation, it's possible to have a custom tile to launch a website/URL in a new tab outside of the launchpad. It's also possible to just load quick links on the custom tile and it's possible too just to load visualization on a tile without any action on Click. Keep exploring and holler if you need any help or want to share feedback.

entry.title: Kickstart Your Developer Journey – Get SAP-Certified in Low-Code/No-Code
entry.content:
New to development? No problem! SAP offers a certification and learning resources that help you build real business apps, workflows, and user experiences — without needing to be a coding expert. Using tools like SAP Build Apps, Work Zone, and Process Automation, you’ll be surprised how much you can do just by dragging, dropping, and configuring.

 

Simple Steps to Get Certified

 

Step 1: Learn at Your Own Pace Follow these beginner-friendly learning paths:

  • SAP Build Apps: Build apps visually without needing to write code. Learn how to connect data, create user-friendly pages, and define app behavior.
  • SAP Build Work Zone: Set up a digital workspace where users can access their apps and content in one place.
  • SAP Build Process Automation: Learn how to build simple workflows and automation tools to save time and reduce manual work.

Step 2: Practice What You Learn You’ll try a real-world simulation using everything you’ve learned. This hands-on practice is required before taking the certification exam and helps boost your confidence.

Step 3: Take the Certification Exam Once you feel ready, book your exam. Passing it proves your skills and gives you an official SAP Certification — a great addition to your resume!

 

Want to Keep Growing?

 

  • Watch tutorials on YouTube from SAP Developer Advocates.
  • Join local SAP CodeJam events to meet and learn from other developers.
  • Subscribe to SAP Learning Hub for more guided learning from SAP experts.

 

Whether you're starting your career or switching to tech, this certification is a great way to get hands-on with real business tools — and build your future.

entry.title: Creating Local Entry Point for Business Solutions in SAP Build Work Zone
entry.content:
Creating Local Entry Point for Business Solutions in SAP Build Work Zone

High-level Overview (TL;DR)

In our previous blog, we introduced multi-tenant support for HTML5 business solutions using a central SAP Build Work Zone  instance. Now, we're excited to announce the new Local Entry Point (LEP) concept of SAP Build Work Zone that simplifies how providers integrate business solutions into SAP BTP. With LEP, providers can now automatically generate a standalone entry-point site on subscription without requiring a Work Zone instance on consumer tenants. This blog explains how to leverage LEP in your multi-tenant business solution.

Introduction

If you haven't read my previous blog discussing business solutions, CDM, and cross-subaccount federation of business applications, I recommend reviewing it first. It provides the context on the underlying concepts of CDM based content provider in SAP Build Work Zone. This blog assumes you understand these concepts and builds on them.

Since Q3 2024, SAP Build Work Zone supported multi-tenant entry points through a centralized instance & managed AppRouter, allowing consumption of HTML5 solutions across multiple subaccounts. Now, the newly released Local Entry Point (LEP) offers even more flexibility, enabling standalone entry points without requiring a Work Zone instance on the consumer side. Instead, the LEP mechanism automatically generates the entry-point site for subscribed SaaS solutions. This significantly reduces the administrative tasks required on the consumer side.

In a nutshell, Work Zone now offers a new service plan under the SAP Build Work Zone standard service (customers and partners). This plan enables SaaS application developers to use Work Zone as a managed reuse service that generates an entry point (standalone site) for the SaaS solution upon subscription in the consumer subaccount. This approach is similar to the existing SAP Cloud Portal Service (launchpad module) and serves as the successor for current usage of Cloud Portal Service in SaaS solutions.

In this blog, I will do a deep dive into how you can leverage LEP as a developer of multi-tenant business solution on SAP BTP, what are the provider subaccount and consumer subaccount administration tasks and finally compare the 2 approaches available in Work Zone to support entry points to SaaS applications.

Developer Flow: Local Entry Point

LEP is build on the principles of cross-subaccount federation of business solutions. Therefore, the sample pre-requisites hold good for LEP implementation as well - Work Zone content definition in cdm.json and ensuring all UI5 app manifests include the unique business solution identifier as sap.cloud.service name.

On a high level, the major difference to central instance based multitenant business solution federation are use of Standalone AppRouter and use of Work Zone standard local-entry-point plan as a reuse service to enable out of the box entry point which is generated upon subscription of the SaaS application on the consumer subaccount.

Pre-requisites

In the following technical deep dive, I would assume that you are familiar with SaaS development best practices on BTP and already have a multi-tenant CAP application where you intend to enable WZ Local Entry Point. In addition, verify the following before moving ahead - 

  1. MTA deployment descriptor is already generated.
  2. Standalone AppRouter is configured.
  3. Deployment configurations for HTML5 applications are in place & HTML5 repo (runtime & host) are added as resources in mta.yaml. 
  4. Every UI5 app manifests have the unique & uniform sap.cloud.service name.
  5. In the target deployment subaccount, ensure that there is an entitlement to SAP Build Work Zone service and service plans standard and local-entry-point.

If you do not have a multi-tenant CAP application already, and you wish to follow along with a sample application (completed code) - refer to this GitHub repo branch. I will be referencing this repo itself for all sample code in the rest of the blog.

Now, let's see detailed step-by-step approach to enable Local Entry Point. 

Assumption: You already have a mult-itenant CAP application & all pre-requisites are met

At first, 8 steps to enable LEP may seem very daunting, but I have broken down the tasks to smaller steps so that we understand what we are doing and why. I hope you will find the setup rather straight forward.

Step 1: Define Work Zone content in CDM format

This step is same as what was discussed in my previous blog. In order to access any Business Solution across subaccounts, the best practice is to expose the Work Zone content definition from provider (source) subaccount in CDM format and adding it as a content provider from consumer (target) subaccount. Same is true for Central Work Zone instance based access to SaaS applications - which are deployed in provider subaccount and consumed from the subscription subaccount - making it essentially a cross-subaccount access scenario. Although the consumer subaccount does not require a Work Zone instance or manual creation of content provider in the new Local Entry Point based approach, the underlying mechanism is still the same. It's just that the CDM based federation is automated under the hood, as part of the SaaS subscription flow and no additional administrative effort is required.

To define the Work Zone content as CDM, please refer to the SAP Build Work Zone documentation on CDM. You can refer to this cdm.json as sample and adapt it to your project requirements. I will not dive into the specifics of schema and how to create the cdm.json but focus on the 2 specific aspects around CDM for LEP - 

  1. LEP only support CDM schema version 3.1 or above and does not support Groups. Hence, the cdm.json should use spaces, pages or workpages.
  2. You must include one (and only one) site entity definition in the cdm.json which will be the default site for the Local Entry Point for the SaaS application and users would be routed to this site on the subscription side. This is also the entity where you can define the subset of site parameters which are usually available in the site administration view of Work Zone. For example, you can define the display config (site view mode, page width, etc.) browser setting (async loading, browser feature access, etc.), user capabilities (personalization, theme selection, locales, enterprise search, etc.) and so on. Given that there is no Work Zone administration capability on the LEP based solutions, these site properties are then provider choice and the only way to influence the Work Zone site behaviour that is generated on the consumer tenant upon subscription of the SaaS application.

Step 2: Setup cdm.json as deployment resource

This is a simple step to add a build script to mta.yaml which will copy the cdm.json from wherever it was added in the project path to the app deployment resources folder so that it's deployed to HTML5 repository and hosted for consumption as content provider from consumer tenants. Below you can find a sample build-parameters section in a mta.yaml and the lines 6 and 7 should be added in already existing mta.yaml - 

build-parameters:
  before-all:
    - builder: custom
      commands:
        - npm ci
        - mkdir -p app/resources
        - cp workzone/cdm.json app/resources/cdm.json
        - npx cds build --production

Step 3: Add Work Zone LEP as reuse resource in mta

Local Entry Point approach is to use Work Zone as a managed reuse service to create a business solution specific Work Zone site on the subscription side without requiring an active Work Zone instance. Hence, it's imperative that we define this as a resource required by the SaaS application. This is done by adding it as an entry in the resources section of the mta.yaml as shown below:

resources:
  - name: incidents-build-workzone-service
    parameters:
      config:
        providerId: ${lepProviderId}-${org}-${space}
        exposureId: incidents.service 
      service-plan: local-entry-point
      service: build-workzone-standard
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service

Few important details to be considered - 

  1. You can choose any yaml-compliant name for the resource, but you would need this later to add dependencies / references from other resources / modules. Hence, best to select something meaningful and easily identifiable as WZ LEP resource.
  2. Exposure ID should be same as the sap.cloud.service (unique identifier for business solutions) that is used in every UI5 app manifest.json.
  3. Service Plan and service should be exactly as in the sample code above.

Step 4: Add dependency to LEP from AppRouter, AppDeployer & MTX module

Once you define the Work Zone LEP resource, it's time to make other components aware of it and hence we will add it as the dependency (requires) from AppRouter, AppDeployer & MTX module. In the mta.yaml, add the LEP resource name under requires for all the aforementioned modules -

requires:
      - name: incidents-build-workzone-service

Step 5: Add Work Zone LEP as required resource for MTX

In addition to adding the dependency in mta.yaml for MTX module, we would also need to add the SaaS registry dependency to Work Zone LEP service so that it can be used in a multi-tenant environment. This is done by adding a service dependency in the mtx/sidecar package.json. Go to mtx/sidecar/package.json and add the following - 

"requires": {
  "html5-host": true,
  "html5-runtime": true,
  "launchpad": {
    "vcap": {
      "label": "build-workzone-standard",
      "plan": "local-entry-point"
    },
    "subscriptionDependency": {
      "uaa": "xsappname"
    }
  }
}

Note that all 3 dependencies are required - html5-host, html5-runtime & launchpad. The vcap.label and vcap.plan for launchpad should remain as in the sample code above.

Step 6: Update the routes for LEP landing page in xs-app

In case of standard development flow and testing without dynamic tiles, custom domain, resource references, etc, following content of xs-app.json under the app/router folder should be sufficient - 

{
  "welcomeFile": "cp.portal/site",
  "routes": [
    {
      "source": "^(.*)$",
      "target": "$1",
      "service": "html5-apps-repo-rt",
      "authenticationType": "xsuaa"
    }
  ]
}

It basically defines the route to the launchpad / landing page which is shown when the user logs in to the Local Entry Point based Work Zone site.

Step 7: Update role template & add role collections for CDM roles in xs-security

Here there is a slight deviation to how roles are managed in cross-subaccount federation and LEP. When an Admin creates the roles manually based on a CDM source, the CDM roles are usually generated as BTP role collections with name in the pattern of ˜<content-provider-name><cdm-role-id>. But, in LEP, the role-collection is explicitly defined in xs-security.json (usually in the root of you MTA project) and it must match the role defined in the cdm.json. This is required for each and every role and there must be a matching role collection in xs-security.json corresponding to each CDM role. The matching role collections in xs-security can be added to the "role-collections" array as per following example -  

...
  "role-collections": [
    {
      "name": "Incidents_LEP",  
      "description": "Grant Administrative access to LEP",
      "role-template-references": [
        "$XSAPPNAME.Token_Exchange",
        "$XSAPPNAME.MultitenancyCallbackRoleTemplate",
        "$XSAPPNAME.support",
        "$XSAPPNAME.admin"
      ]
    }
  ]
 ...

Step 8: Deploy to provider subaccount, subscribe from consumer subaccount & test

This step is not specific to LEP as such and an obvious step for any development flow. In order to test the LEP based entry point, developers must deploy it to a dev subaccount which will act as a provider subaccount. Please note that currently, developers cannot test LEP based flow locally, which is otherwise supported in CAP based multi-tenant applications.

To deploy in Cloud Foundry, follow the standard mbt build followed by cf deploy <mtar-name>.mtar as explained in this tutorial.

Admin Flow

Provider Administration

The provider (app development team admin) manages the subaccount including adding entitlement to Local Entry Point plan for Work Zone standard service, adds trusted domains and monitors subscriptions.

As a prerequisite to deploy LEP based multi-tenant application on SAP BTP, administrators must maintain the entitlement to the Local Entry Point plan as seen here -

Once successfully deployed the service instances should show up in the respective BTP cockpit. Notice the Work Zone service can be seen as subscribed with plan as local-entry-point which should have 2 bindings as defined in MTA - MTX and the App Router applications. It should not be possible to subscribe to this solution from any subaccount and test the end-to-end flow.

You will also find the applications in the space where the SaaS application was deployed and perhaps a good place to observe that the AppRouter, MTX and CAP backend service are up and running.

On the provider subaccount, administrators can also find all subscriptions in the SaaS registry (can be accessed from Instance and Subscriptions in BTP cockpit) - 

Tenant specific route creation:

In development setup, in order to enable the subscriber tenant routes to access the provider content, Provider subaccount administrator must also create a tenant specific route for each subscriber. Please refer to this create tenant-specific route in Cloud Foundry guide. This route creation is usually done post every subscription and you would require the host name of the subscriber tenant to create it. The route should be mapped to the AppRouter application instance on the provider as well. Once done, users with required role collection assignment on the consumer subaccount would be able to access the Local Entry Point site.

Consumer Administration

Consumers have minimal administration effort: post subscription, they only need to assign users to role collections. No other Work Zone administrative tasks are required or possible.

SaaS application can be subscribed from SAP BTP Service Marketplace and once done the subscription should be visible in the Instances and Subscriptions in SAP BTP cockpit (see below for example). 

You can extract the URL to Local Entry Point either from this subscription link itself or from the button on the top right on the subscription details view (see above) - which you can then test or provide to users. 

Please note, this wouldn't work (404 Not found error) until the Provider Admin creates the tenant specific route as mentioned in the Provider Amin flow under section "Tenant Specific Route Creation". Once done, user should be able to launch the Local Entry Point site and navigate into the UI5 apps within the SaaS solution using the application URL provided the requisite role collections are already assigned - 

If you have keen eyes, you would have noticed that there's a UI integration card (declarative list type) in the site runtime screenshot above. I left it in intentionally as a teaser for what you can expect in the next blog 😉 

Central Instance vs. Local Entry Point – Key Differences

Now that there are 2 parallel options to achieve entry point for SaaS applications on SAP BTP using Work Zone, let us first demystify the options by comparing them side by side - 

Feature

Central WZ Instance

Local Entry Point

User access

Single business site for all SaaS solutions

Each SaaS solution provides its own business site

Consumer-side Work Zone Instance

Required (Standard or Advanced)

Not Required

AppRouter Management

Managed AppRouter (out of the box)

Standalone AppRouter (extensible)

Site Administration

Administered by Consumer; full Work Zone instance on consumer subaccount.

No Consumer Administration; Provider Managed as site properties in CDM

Layout Customization

Customizable by Consumers (administrator defines spaces/pages with federated business apps)

Layout Fixed by Provider (CDM-defined)
Groups are not supported

Based on these differences, one can the best approach to enable entry point to their multi-tenant business solution. 

Following 2 diagrams will help clarify the differences between these 2 options further, while presenting different subscription subaccount setup possibilities.

 

Conclusion

The Local Entry Point feature simplifies multi-tenant deployments, improving the flexibility and usability of SAP Build Work Zone integrations. Check out further details in the official documentation. Please review the current restrictions (updated regularly) to map your requirements before adopting Local Entry Point approach. Another excellent resource would be SAP BTP developer’s guide to implement multitenancy using Local Entry Point option.

Looking forward to your feedback and questions!
